summary: McKenzie shows before-and-after screenshots of his Bingo Card Creator software after purchasing stock icons for $29.95. He demonstrates how the visual upgrade from version 1.03 to 1.04 makes the interface look more colorful and professional. He emphasizes that this particular screenshot is his most important sales asset, placed above the fold on his main page. He also notes a side benefit: changing the card preview to white backgrounds better reflects printed output.
key_points: A $29.95 investment in stock icons can meaningfully improve the perceived quality of software
The most prominent screenshot on a product page is a critical sales tool and deserves investment
Visual polish makes software look more substantial to potential customers, independent of functionality
Side-effect improvements (white card preview matching print output) can be kept when they enhance both aesthetics and usability
money_quotes: This is the screenshot which directly sells my program more than any other.
Its my interface looking at its best and brightest and it hints to the teachers exactly how much use they can get out of my program.
Now doesn't that look more colorful and vibrant?
I will keep that interface element because it makes my program look more substantial and because it greatly simplifies the workflow.
I took advantage of icons-icons's August sale to get a pile of stock icons for $29.95.
key_insight: Small, inexpensive visual improvements like stock icons can disproportionately improve how professional and substantial your software appears to potential customers.
